examples.title: DVR Initialization Algorithm
examples.description: Shows how each router initializes its routing table at startup
examples.code:
PROCEDURE InitializeRoutingTable()
BEGIN
 FOR each network n connected to router R DO
 distance[n] ← 0
 nextHop[n] ← R
 metric[n] ← 0
 END FOR
 
 FOR each destination d in internetwork DO
 IF d is not directly connected THEN
 distance[d] ← INFINITY
 nextHop[d] ← UNKNOWN
 metric[d] ← INFINITY
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 RETURN routingTable
END
examples.explanation: Each router initializes by setting distance to directly connected networks as 0 and all other destinations as infinity. This is the starting point before any routing updates are exchanged.

examples.title: Bellman-Ford Update Algorithm
examples.description: Core algorithm for updating routing table based on neighbor's distance vector
examples.code:
PROCEDURE UpdateRoutingTable(neighbor, neighborDistanceVector)
BEGIN
 FOR each destination d IN neighborDistanceVector DO
 newCost ← linkCost(R, neighbor) + neighborDistanceVector[d]
 
 IF newCost < routingTable[d].metric THEN
 routingTable[d].metric ← newCost
 routingTable[d].nextHop ← neighbor
 routeChanged ← TRUE
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 IF routeChanged = TRUE AND triggeredUpdateEnabled THEN
 SendUpdateToAllNeighbors()
 END IF
 
 RETURN routingTable
END
examples.explanation: When a router receives a distance vector from a neighbor, it applies the Bellman-Ford equation to find shorter paths. If a better route is found, the table is updated and triggered updates may be sent.

examples.title: RIP Timer Management
examples.description: Shows the timer mechanisms used in RIP protocol
examples.code:
PROCEDURE StartRIPTimers()
BEGIN
 updateTimer ← 30 // seconds
 invalidTimer ← 180 // seconds
 holddownTimer ← 60 // seconds
 flushTimer ← 240 // seconds
 
 WHILE routerActive DO
 updateTimer--
 invalidTimer--
 holddownTimer--
 flushTimer--
 
 IF updateTimer = 0 THEN
 SendFullRoutingTable()
 updateTimer ← 30
 END IF
 
 IF invalidTimer = 0 THEN
 route.metric ← INFINITY (16)
 startHolddown()
 END IF
 
 IF flushTimer = 0 THEN
 RemoveRouteFromTable()
 END IF
 
 Wait(1 second)
 END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: RIP uses four timers to manage routing information freshness and prevent stale routes from being used indefinitely.

examples.title: Split Horizon Implementation
examples.description: Demonstrates how split horizon is implemented to prevent routing loops
examples.code:
PROCEDURE SendUpdateWithSplitHorizon(neighbor)
BEGIN
 updateMessage ← EMPTY
 
 FOR each route IN routingTable DO
 // Do not advertise route back to the neighbor from which it was learned
 IF route.nextHop ≠ neighbor THEN
 AddRouteToUpdate(updateMessage, route)
 ELSE IF route.metric = INFINITY THEN
 // Poison reverse: advertise as unreachable
 AddRouteToUpdate(updateMessage, route.destination, INFINITY)
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 SendToNeighbor(neighbor, updateMessage)
END
examples.explanation: Split horizon prevents routing loops by not advertising routes back to the neighbor. Poison reverse goes further by explicitly marking such routes as unreachable (infinite metric).
